content.text: Structured logging is the practice of emitting machine-readable, field-keyed records at every meaningful event in an agent's execution: when a run starts, when a tool is invoked, when the LLM responds, when an error is caught, and when the session ends. Unlike plain text logs or print statements, structured logs carry a consistent set of fields — run_id, session_id, component, timestamp, duration_ms — that let operators search, correlate, alert, and reconstruct any agent session without re-running it.
What it is

Logging in agentic AI systems is the act of recording discrete, timestamped events that describe what the agent did and what happened as a result. At its simplest level, this means calling Python's built-in logging module or a library like structlog to emit a line per event. In production, each line is a JSON object rather than a human-readable string, making it trivially queryable in Elasticsearch, Datadog, Grafana Loki, CloudWatch Logs, or any other log aggregation platform.

A log event in an agent context is not the same as a web-server access log. Agent runs are long-lived, multi-step processes that fan out across services — an orchestrator calls an LLM, the LLM selects a tool, the tool hits a database or external API, the result returns to the orchestrator for the next step. Every hop in that chain must carry the same run_id so all events can be stitched together in a single chronological view. Logs provide the low-latency, high-volume complement to distributed traces: traces give you the call graph; logs give you the why — what decision was made, what payload was sent, what error occurred.

Structlog and Python's standard logging library are the two most common choices. Structlog adds a processor pipeline on top of the standard library: each processor is a function that transforms the event dict before it is rendered. Processors can add timestamps, rename keys, redact secrets, or format the dict as JSON. The standard library alone can be configured with a JSON formatter (python-json-logger or a custom Formatter subclass) to achieve similar output, at the cost of less composable configuration.
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems fail in ways that are invisible without logs. A model that loops fifty times before giving up, a tool that silently returns an empty result instead of raising an exception, a rate-limit retry that doubles latency — none of these show up as HTTP 500 errors on a reverse proxy. They only become visible if the agent itself emits the right events.

Logs are the primary debugging channel in production environments where attaching a debugger is impossible and reproducing the exact state is impractical. When a user reports that the agent 'deleted the wrong file', support engineers search for run_id:r-9281 event:tool_call tool:delete_file in the log store and get a sub-second timeline: which model version was running, what the tool arguments were, whether the call succeeded, and what subsequent actions followed. Without structured logs, that reconstruction requires replaying the entire session from scratch or reading raw stdout output line by line.

Logs also serve compliance and audit requirements. Regulations like SOC 2, GDPR data retention mandates, and enterprise security policies often require that autonomous system actions — particularly those that write, delete, or send data — be permanently recorded in an immutable audit trail. Structured logs fed into an append-only store satisfy this requirement cleanly.
Benefits
Instant timeline reconstruction
— filter by run_id to get an ordered list of every event in a session, spanning multiple services and tool calls.
Metric derivation
— count tool_failed events per minute, compute p95 LLM latency, or track run completion rates using log-based metrics without a separate instrumentation pass.
Alerting
— set threshold alerts on ERROR rate or stuck-run detection (no run_end seen within N minutes of run_start) directly in the log store.
Cross-service correlation
— propagate run_id through HTTP headers and queue messages so orchestrator, tool worker, and database logs all appear in one search result.
Audit trail
— permanent, tamper-evident record of autonomous actions for compliance, incident response, and post-mortems.
Low overhead
— structured logs at INFO level add negligible latency compared to LLM inference time (milliseconds vs. seconds).
Trade-offs
Volume and cost
— agents that call many tools in tight loops can emit thousands of log lines per run. Log ingestion and storage costs scale with volume; over-logging is common.
PII and secret exposure
— agent inputs often contain user data, API keys, or bearer tokens. Logging them at INFO level creates a compliance and security liability.
Noise obscures signal
— when every log line is INFO, finding actual errors requires reading everything. Correct level discipline is critical.
Operational complexity
— centralised logging requires a log shipper, an aggregation backend, retention policies, access controls, and alerting rules. This is non-trivial infrastructure.
Latency tail in high-frequency logging
— synchronous log writes to slow handlers (e.g. direct HTTP to a remote API) can add latency. Async handlers or local buffering are required.
How it works
The event pipeline

When a log call is made — log.info('tool_start', run_id=run_id, tool='search') — the library constructs an event dict from the keyword arguments and the bound context. The dict passes through a chain of processors in order: each processor receives the dict, may modify it, and returns it to the next processor. At the end of the chain, a renderer serialises the dict to a string (typically JSON) and passes it to a handler — a file, stderr, or an async queue. The handler writes the string to its destination.

Context binding is the key mechanism that makes run-level correlation work. structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(run_id=run_id) stores the run_id in Python's ContextVar, which is automatically isolated per asyncio task. Every subsequent log call in that task — regardless of which module it comes from — will include run_id in its output without the caller needing to pass it explicitly. At the end of the run, clear_contextvars() removes the binding.
Level semantics for agents

Log levels in agent systems carry specific semantic contracts. Violating them makes logs useless: if every event is INFO, searching for errors requires reading everything; if retries are ERROR, PagerDuty alerts fire on every transient failure.
DEBUG
— full LLM request/response bodies, raw tool arguments, internal state dumps. Never enabled in production by default.
INFO
— run_start, run_end, tool_start, tool_done, llm_call with token counts and latency. The steady-state production level.
WARNING
— retry attempt, rate limit hit, deprecated tool used, context length approaching limit. Actionable but not urgent.
ERROR
— tool exception, LLM API failure, policy violation, unexpected state. Triggers alerting.
CRITICAL
— data loss risk, auth bypass attempt, kill switch triggered. Requires immediate human response.
Redaction and field discipline

Redaction must happen before the event dict reaches the renderer. A structlog processor that iterates known sensitive keys and replaces their values with '***' runs early in the pipeline so no downstream handler ever sees the raw value. When tool arguments may contain user-supplied text, log the argument keys, not the values: arg_keys=['query', 'path'] instead of args={'query': 'user secret ...'}. Payload truncation handles large values — truncate strings to 500 characters and log an is_truncated flag.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full logging pipeline for a production agentic AI service. The agent loop emits structured events to a local logger, which applies redaction, timestamps, and JSON encoding before handing off to a log shipper. The shipper buffers and forwards to one or more backends. A separate append-only audit stream captures destructive tool actions.

attrs.alt: Structured logging architecture for agentic AI: agent loop → structured logger → log shipper → ELK/Datadog/S3 backends, plus a separate audit log

content.text: The critical design point is that run_id propagates through every layer. The agent loop binds it in contextvars at run start; the logger includes it automatically in every event; the log shipper preserves it as a field; and the backend indexes it. A single query run_id:r-9281 returns all events — from orchestrator, tool workers, and sub-agents — in one result set.
When to use it

Structured logging should be active in every production agent service from day one — it is not something to add after problems appear, because by that point there is no history to query. The decision space is around what to log at which level and where to send it, not whether to log.
Production services
— always use JSON structured logs at INFO+. Ship to a centralised aggregator. Alert on ERROR rate and stuck runs.
Local development
— use DEBUG level with console (human-readable) renderer. Log full LLM messages to understand model behaviour during prompt engineering.
Destructive tool calls
— always write to a separate append-only audit log, regardless of normal log level configuration.
High-frequency inner loops
— sample or aggregate rather than logging every iteration. Log summary statistics at the end of the loop.
When NOT to log

— do not log per-token streaming events in production. Do not log raw HTTP bodies from external APIs (they may include credentials). Do not log user PII unless there is explicit legal basis and the log store is appropriately access-controlled.
How to implement
Step 1: configure structlog at application startup
Configure the processor chain once, at the entry point of the application, before any other module imports a logger. Reconfiguring mid-run causes inconsistent output.

# logging_config.py — call configure_logging() before anything else
import logging
import sys
import structlog

def configure_logging(level: str = "INFO", render_json: bool = True) -> None:
    """Wire up structlog with optional JSON rendering.

    Call this exactly once at application startup.
    level: 'DEBUG' | 'INFO' | 'WARNING' | 'ERROR'
    render_json: True for production JSON; False for human-readable dev output.
    """
    shared_processors = [
        structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
        structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso", utc=True),
        _redact_processor,
    ]

    if render_json:
        renderer = structlog.processors.JSONRenderer()
    else:
        renderer = structlog.dev.ConsoleRenderer(colors=True)

    structlog.configure(
        processors=shared_processors + [renderer],
        wrapper_class=structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger,
        context_class=dict,
        logger_factory=structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory(),
        cache_logger_on_first_use=True,
    )

    # Also configure stdlib so third-party libraries use the same level
    logging.basicConfig(
        format="%(message)s",
        stream=sys.stdout,
        level=getattr(logging, level.upper()),
    )

# Sensitive field names to redact
_REDACT_KEYS = frozenset({
    "api_key", "secret", "password", "token", "authorization",
    "x-api-key", "bearer", "access_token", "refresh_token",
})

def _redact_processor(logger, method, event_dict: dict) -> dict:
    """Redact known sensitive keys before any renderer sees them."""
    for key in list(event_dict.keys()):
        if key.lower() in _REDACT_KEYS:
            event_dict[key] = "***REDACTED***"
    return event_dict
Step 2: bind context at run start

import structlog
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

log = structlog.get_logger(__name__)

@asynccontextmanager
async def agent_run_context(run_id: str, session_id: str, user_id: str):
    """Async context manager that binds run-level fields for all logs."""
    structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(
        run_id=run_id,
        session_id=session_id,
        user_id=user_id,   # hash or opaque id — not raw email
    )
    log.info("run_start")
    try:
        yield
        log.info("run_end", status="success")
    except Exception as exc:
        log.error("run_error", error_type=type(exc).__name__, error=str(exc))
        raise
    finally:
        structlog.contextvars.clear_contextvars()
Step 3: instrument tool calls

import time
import structlog

log = structlog.get_logger(__name__)

async def invoke_tool(tool_name: str, args: dict) -> dict:
    """Wrap every tool call with structured start/end/error logs."""
    # Log keys, not values — values may contain user data
    log.info(
        "tool_start",
        tool=tool_name,
        arg_keys=list(args.keys()),
    )
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        result = await _execute_tool(tool_name, args)
        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
        log.info(
            "tool_done",
            tool=tool_name,
            duration_ms=round(elapsed_ms, 1),
            result_keys=list(result.keys()) if isinstance(result, dict) else None,
        )
        return result
    except RateLimitError as exc:
        log.warning("tool_rate_limited", tool=tool_name, retry_after=exc.retry_after)
        raise
    except Exception as exc:
        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
        log.error(
            "tool_failed",
            tool=tool_name,
            duration_ms=round(elapsed_ms, 1),
            error_type=type(exc).__name__,
            error=str(exc)[:200],  # truncate long messages
        )
        raise
Examples
Example 1: minimal structured logger with stdlib
This example uses only the Python standard library with the python-json-logger package, suitable when a dependency on structlog is not desired.

# pip install python-json-logger
import logging
import json
import uuid
from pythonjsonlogger import jsonlogger

def build_logger(name: str, level: str = "INFO") -> logging.Logger:
    logger = logging.getLogger(name)
    logger.setLevel(getattr(logging, level))

    handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    formatter = jsonlogger.JsonFormatter(
        fmt="%(asctime)s %(name)s %(levelname)s %(message)s",
        datefmt="%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S",
    )
    handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    logger.addHandler(handler)
    return logger

# --- usage ---
logger = build_logger("agent.orchestrator")

run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())[:8]

# Attach run_id as extra fields
extra = {"run_id": run_id, "session_id": "sess-42"}

logger.info("run_start", extra=extra)

# Simulate a tool call
logger.info("tool_start", extra={**extra, "tool": "web_search", "arg_keys": ["query"]})
# ... tool executes ...
logger.info("tool_done", extra={**extra, "tool": "web_search", "duration_ms": 310})

logger.info("run_end", extra={**extra, "status": "success"})

# Sample output (pretty-printed here for readability):
# {
#   "asctime": "2026-07-10T14:22:01",
#   "name": "agent.orchestrator",
#   "levelname": "INFO",
#   "message": "tool_done",
#   "run_id": "a1b2c3d4",
#   "session_id": "sess-42",
#   "tool": "web_search",
#   "duration_ms": 310
# }
Example 2: structlog with async context and audit sidecar
This more realistic example shows context propagation across an async agent loop, with a separate audit logger for destructive operations.

# pip install structlog
import asyncio
import time
import uuid
import structlog
import logging
import sys

# --- setup (call once at startup) ---
structlog.configure(
    processors=[
        structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso", utc=True),
        structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
    ],
    wrapper_class=structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger,
    context_class=dict,
    logger_factory=structlog.stdlib.LoggerFactory(),
)
logging.basicConfig(format="%(message)s", stream=sys.stdout, level=logging.INFO)

# Separate audit logger — in production, point this at an immutable sink
audit_log = logging.getLogger("agent.audit")
audit_handler = logging.FileHandler("audit.jsonl")
audit_log.addHandler(audit_handler)
audit_log.setLevel(logging.INFO)

log = structlog.get_logger("agent.orchestrator")

# --- agent loop ---
async def run_agent(goal: str) -> str:
    run_id = uuid.uuid4().hex[:10]
    structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(run_id=run_id, goal_preview=goal[:60])
    log.info("run_start")

    steps = [
        ("web_search", {"query": goal}, False),
        ("write_file",  {"path": "/tmp/result.txt", "content": "..."}, True),  # destructive
    ]

    for tool_name, args, is_destructive in steps:
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        log.info("tool_start", tool=tool_name, arg_keys=list(args.keys()))

        # Audit destructive actions to the immutable sink
        if is_destructive:
            audit_log.info(
                '{"event": "destructive_tool", "tool": "%s", "args_keys": %s, "run_id": "%s"}'
                % (tool_name, list(args.keys()), run_id)
            )

        await asyncio.sleep(0.05)  # simulate tool latency
        elapsed = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
        log.info("tool_done", tool=tool_name, duration_ms=round(elapsed, 1))

    log.info("run_end", status="success", total_tools=len(steps))
    structlog.contextvars.clear_contextvars()
    return "done"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(run_agent("Summarise the Q4 earnings report"))
Real-world example

Consider a customer-support agent that handles refund requests. Each run touches four tools: lookup_order, check_policy, process_refund, and send_email. The service runs on Kubernetes with 20 replicas, handling roughly 500 concurrent sessions. All pods ship logs to Datadog via the Datadog agent sidecar.

When a refund is incorrectly rejected, the support engineer enters the Datadog log explorer and queries: @run_id:r-7f3a @tool:check_policy. Within two seconds the query returns the exact event: the policy check returned 'ineligible' because the order_date field was parsed in UTC but compared against a local-time cutoff. The engineer can see the exact field values (order_date, cutoff_date, timezone) because those were logged at INFO level — they are not PII. The user's email and name were never logged; only user_id (an opaque UUID) appears.

A Datadog monitor alerts on (@event:tool_failed) over a 5-minute rolling window exceeding 50 occurrences. When the payment gateway goes down, this alert fires within five minutes, long before user complaints reach the support queue. The on-call engineer uses the log timeline to confirm all failures are in process_refund and rolls back the gateway integration without needing to check every affected run individually.

# Excerpt: what each log line looks like in Datadog for the refund agent

# run_start
{
  "timestamp": "2026-07-10T09:14:22.103Z",
  "level": "info",
  "event": "run_start",
  "run_id": "r-7f3a",
  "session_id": "sess-881",
  "user_id": "u-00042",
  "component": "agent.orchestrator"
}

# tool_done — note: no user content in the log
{
  "timestamp": "2026-07-10T09:14:22.857Z",
  "level": "info",
  "event": "tool_done",
  "run_id": "r-7f3a",
  "tool": "check_policy",
  "duration_ms": 754,
  "result": "ineligible",
  "order_date": "2026-06-30T23:58:00Z",
  "cutoff_date": "2026-07-01T00:00:00+02:00"
}

# run_end
{
  "timestamp": "2026-07-10T09:14:23.512Z",
  "level": "info",
  "event": "run_end",
  "run_id": "r-7f3a",
  "status": "success",
  "total_tools": 4,
  "total_duration_ms": 1409
}
Common mistakes
Using print() in production

print() writes unstructured text to stdout with no level, no timestamp, and no fields. Container runtimes collect stdout but there is no way to filter by run_id, set alert thresholds on error rate, or ingest it into a log aggregator without parsing fragile strings.

# Wrong
print(f"Tool {tool_name} failed: {exc}")

# Right
log.error("tool_failed", tool=tool_name, error=str(exc)[:200])
Logging PII and secrets at INFO
It is tempting to log the full user request for debugging. Doing so creates a GDPR / CCPA liability, exposes data to anyone with log access, and often violates enterprise data handling policies.

# Wrong — logs user email and full prompt at INFO
log.info("run_start", user_email=user.email, prompt=user_prompt)

# Right — log opaque IDs and prompt metadata only
log.info("run_start", user_id=user.id, prompt_len=len(user_prompt))
Missing run_id propagation

When a tool worker runs in a separate service or async task without receiving the run_id, its logs are orphaned — they appear in the log store but cannot be correlated with the orchestrator's timeline.

# Wrong — tool worker logs with no run_id
async def handle_task(task: Task):
    log.info("task_start", tool=task.tool)  # no run_id bound

# Right — propagate run_id into every downstream context
async def handle_task(task: Task):
    structlog.contextvars.bind_contextvars(run_id=task.run_id)
    log.info("task_start", tool=task.tool)
Logging inside tight inner loops

Emitting one log line per token in a streaming response or per iteration of a search loop generates orders of magnitude more volume than useful events. This inflates ingestion costs and makes it harder to find meaningful events.

# Wrong — one log per token
async for token in llm.stream(prompt):
    log.debug("token", value=token)

# Right — log start/end of the LLM call; collect tokens silently
t0 = time.perf_counter()
log.info("llm_start", model=model_id, prompt_tokens=num_tokens(prompt))
response = "".join([token async for token in llm.stream(prompt)])
log.info(
    "llm_done",
    model=model_id,
    duration_ms=round((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000, 1),
    completion_tokens=num_tokens(response),
)
Best practices
One configuration module
— call configure_logging() exactly once at the application entry point. Never call logging.basicConfig() in a library module; never scatter structlog.configure() calls.
Bind context, do not pass it
— use structlog.contextvars to bind run_id, session_id, and user_id at the request boundary. Every log call in that async task inherits them automatically.
Log events, not messages
— use a short machine-readable event name as the log message ('tool_start', 'llm_done') and put human details in fields. This makes aggregation and alerting trivial.
Redact before render
— put a redaction processor early in the pipeline so it runs before JSONRenderer. Never rely on post-processing in the log store to redact secrets.
Separate audit from operational logs
— route destructive actions (file writes, database mutations, emails sent) to an append-only audit sink with its own retention policy.
Pair with OpenTelemetry trace_id
— if the service uses OTel tracing, add trace_id and span_id to every log event so logs and traces are linkable in platforms like Grafana Tempo or Datadog APM.
Test log output in CI
— add a test that runs a sample agent action and asserts that the expected log events (run_start, tool_done, run_end) appear in the output. Catches configuration regressions.
Performance & scalability

Under normal INFO-level logging, the overhead of structured logging is negligible relative to LLM inference. A structlog call with five fields takes roughly 5–20 microseconds for JSON serialisation plus the cost of the underlying file or stream write. LLM inference takes hundreds of milliseconds to seconds, so logging adds less than 0.01% overhead for typical event rates.

At high event rates — for example, logging every step of a multi-agent swarm with 100 concurrent runs — the bottleneck shifts to the handler. A synchronous StreamHandler writing to stdout can become a bottleneck if the write rate exceeds the OS buffer capacity. The solution is to use Python's QueueHandler with a QueueListener in a dedicated thread, which decouples log emission from log writing:

import logging
import logging.handlers
import queue

def configure_async_handler(log_queue: queue.Queue) -> None:
    """Use QueueHandler so log calls return immediately."""    queue_handler = logging.handlers.QueueHandler(log_queue)
    root_logger = logging.getLogger()
    root_logger.addHandler(queue_handler)

    # A real JSON handler on the listener thread
    real_handler = logging.StreamHandler()
    # ... set formatter ...
    listener = logging.handlers.QueueListener(log_queue, real_handler, respect_handler_level=True)
    listener.start()
    # Call listener.stop() at shutdown

Log shippers like Filebeat and Fluentd handle batching and retry automatically. Configure a local buffer (in memory or on disk) so that a brief outage of the aggregation backend does not cause log loss. For very high-volume deployments, consider sampling DEBUG logs: log every Nth event in tight loops, or use adaptive sampling that increases verbosity only when ERROR rate rises above a threshold.
Tooling & ecosystem
structlog

The de facto standard for structured logging in Python. Its processor pipeline model is composable: add processors for context merging, redaction, callsite information, or custom enrichment without touching existing code. Integrates with stdlib logging so third-party libraries that use the standard logger still appear in the same output stream.
python-json-logger
A lightweight JSON formatter for the stdlib logging module. Less powerful than structlog's processor model but requires no additional abstraction. Suitable for teams that want minimal dependencies.
OpenTelemetry Logs SDK

The emerging standard for vendor-neutral telemetry. The OTel Logs SDK for Python emits log records that carry trace_id and span_id, enabling log-to-trace correlation in any OTel-compatible backend. Requires more setup than structlog but provides a unified signal across logs, metrics, and traces.

# OpenTelemetry logging bridge — attach OTel handler to stdlib logger
from opentelemetry._logs import set_logger_provider
from opentelemetry.sdk._logs import LoggerProvider, LoggingHandler
from opentelemetry.sdk._logs.export import BatchLogRecordProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc._log_exporter import OTLPLogExporter

provider = LoggerProvider()
provider.add_log_record_processor(
    BatchLogRecordProcessor(OTLPLogExporter(endpoint="http://otelcol:4317"))
)
set_logger_provider(provider)

handler = LoggingHandler(level=logging.DEBUG, logger_provider=provider)
logging.getLogger().addHandler(handler)
# Now all stdlib log calls are exported as OTel log records
Log aggregation backends
Elasticsearch / OpenSearch + Kibana (ELK) — full-text and field search, dashboards, index-level retention.
Grafana Loki — label-based log streams, Prometheus-compatible alerting, low storage cost for high-volume logs.
Datadog Logs — SaaS, integrated with APM traces and metrics, log-based monitors, RBAC on log indexes.
AWS CloudWatch Logs — native AWS integration, filter patterns for metric derivation, log archiving to S3.
Google Cloud Logging — native GCP integration, structured log viewer, export to BigQuery for analytics.
Comparison with alternatives
Logging is one of three primary observability signals alongside metrics and distributed traces. Each has a different cost, latency profile, and best use case for agent debugging.
Signal
Granularity
Query model
Cost
Best for agents
Structured logs
Event-level (every tool call)
Field search, full-text
Medium — per-line ingestion
Timeline reconstruction, error details, audit
Distributed traces
Span-level (call graph)
Trace ID lookup, service map
Low-medium — sampled
Latency breakdown, service dependency mapping
Metrics
Aggregated time-series
PromQL / Datadog metrics
Low — pre-aggregated
Alerting, dashboards, SLO tracking
print() / stdout
Unstructured text
grep (no field search)
Free but unmanageable
Quick local debugging only — not for production
OpenTelemetry (all signals)
All three unified
Unified query (Grafana)
Varies by backend
Cross-signal correlation in large deployments

In practice, the three signals are complementary. A Datadog monitor alerts on a spike in tool_failed log events. The engineer opens the trace for one of the failing runs to see which span is slow. They query the logs for that span's trace_id to read the exact error message and tool arguments. No single signal alone provides that diagnostic path.
Security & reliability
Access control

Agent logs may contain partial user inputs, tool results, and system prompts. Access to the log store must be restricted to operations and engineering personnel with a legitimate need. In regulated environments, implement role-based access so customer support cannot read logs from other tenants' runs.
Secret exfiltration via logs

A log injection attack is possible when user-supplied content is logged without sanitisation and the log store parses structured records from the content field. If a user submits a crafted input that looks like a JSON log event — e.g., containing "level":"error","event":"auth_bypass" — it may inject false records into a naive log parser. Mitigation: always log user content as a string value inside a named field (user_input=...), never interpolated directly into the log message.
Immutable audit logs

For compliance, route tool_called events for destructive operations to an append-only store. AWS S3 with Object Lock, Google Cloud Storage with retention policies, or Azure Immutable Blob Storage all support WORM (write once, read many) semantics. The audit log should be in a different account or project from the production service so a compromised service account cannot tamper with its own audit trail.
Log reliability

Do not let logging failures crash the agent. Wrap handlers in try/except or use Python's logging.raiseExceptions = False in production. Configure the log shipper with a local disk buffer so transient network outages do not cause log loss. Set a maximum buffer size and decide whether to drop or block when the buffer is full — dropping is usually preferable to blocking the agent loop.
Key takeaways
Emit structured JSON log events — not plain text or print() calls — for every meaningful agent lifecycle event: run start, tool invocation, LLM call, and run end.
Bind run_id and session_id into structlog.contextvars at the start of every run so every log call in that async task automatically carries the correlation fields.
Respect log level semantics: INFO for lifecycle events and latencies, WARNING for retries and rate limits, ERROR for exceptions and policy violations, DEBUG for full payloads in development only.
Redact secrets and PII before the renderer sees them; log argument keys instead of values when arguments may contain sensitive user data.
Maintain a separate append-only audit log for destructive tool actions; route it to a WORM-capable store with its own retention policy and access controls.
At production scale, use an async queue handler (QueueHandler + QueueListener) so log serialisation does not block the agent event loop.
Pair log events with OpenTelemetry trace_id when using distributed tracing so logs and traces are linkable in dashboards and alert workflows.
Logs complement but do not replace distributed traces and metrics: use all three signals together for complete agent observability.
